title: Quiz: Classical Credit Scoring
description: Test your understanding of traditional credit scoring methods and their limitations

questions.question: What is the primary output of a credit scoring model?

options.text: The exact loan amount to approve
A probability of default or creditworthiness score

questions.explanation: Credit scoring models output a probability of default or a numerical score representing creditworthiness. Loan amounts and interest rates are determined by subsequent business rules using this score as input.

questions.question: Why is logistic regression still widely used for credit scoring despite newer methods?

options.text: It always has the highest accuracy
It is interpretable and coefficients can be explained to regulators

questions.explanation: Logistic regression is valued for its interpretability. Each coefficient can be directly explained (e.g., 'higher debt ratio increases default probability'), which is crucial for regulatory compliance and explaining decisions to customers.

questions.question: What does the KS (Kolmogorov-Smirnov) statistic measure in credit scoring?

options.text: The maximum separation between cumulative good and bad distributions
The number of features in the model

questions.explanation: The KS statistic measures the maximum difference between the cumulative distribution of defaults and non-defaults across the score range. It indicates how well the model separates good borrowers from bad borrowers.

questions.question: In credit scoring, what is the relationship between Gini coefficient and AUC?
questions.explanation: The Gini coefficient equals 2 × AUC - 1. An AUC of 0.5 (random) gives Gini of 0, while a perfect AUC of 1.0 gives Gini of 1.0. Both measure discrimination ability.

questions.question: Random forests are less prone to overfitting than single decision trees because they average predictions across many trees trained on different bootstrap samples.
questions.explanation: True. Random forests reduce overfitting through ensemble averaging. Each tree sees a different bootstrap sample and subset of features, reducing variance while maintaining the ability to capture non-linear relationships.

questions.question: What is a key computational limitation of kernel methods (like SVM) for credit scoring?

options.text: Kernel computation scales O(n²) with training samples
They only work with linear relationships
They require exact labels, not probabilities

questions.explanation: Kernel methods require computing the kernel matrix, which is O(n²) in the number of training samples. This becomes prohibitive for datasets with millions of loan applications.

questions.question: Why is class imbalance a significant challenge in credit scoring?

options.text: There are always equal numbers of defaults and non-defaults
Default rates are typically low, so models are biased toward predicting non-default
Class imbalance only affects unsupervised learning
It makes the data too easy to model

questions.explanation: Default rates are typically 1-10%, meaning models can achieve high accuracy by predicting 'no default' for everyone. This is useless for credit risk. Special techniques (oversampling, cost-sensitive learning) are needed.

questions.question: Which metrics are specifically important for credit scoring model evaluation? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: ROC-AUC, KS Statistic, and Gini Coefficient are all standard metrics for evaluating credit scoring models as they measure discrimination between good and bad borrowers. R-squared is for regression problems, not classification.
